learning_objectives: Define culture and identify its visible and invisible components
Explain how cultural identity shapes perception and behavior
Distinguish between ethnocentrism and cultural relativism
readings: Cultural Anthropology: A Toolkit for a Global Age Ch. 1-2 by Guest
The Danger of a Single Story (Chimamanda Ngozi Adichie, transcript)

lecture_notes.introduction: Culture is the lens through which we see the world. It shapes what we consider normal, beautiful, polite, and important. Understanding culture is the first step toward understanding both others and ourselves.
key_concepts: Culture defined: shared beliefs, values, norms, and practices of a group
The cultural iceberg: visible behaviors above the surface, invisible values and assumptions below
Cultural identity: the intersection of nationality, ethnicity, language, religion, class, gender
Ethnocentrism: judging other cultures by the standards of your own
Cultural relativism: understanding a culture on its own terms
lecture_notes.demonstration: The cultural iceberg exercise: students list visible and invisible elements of their own cultural identity

review.value: Above the waterline: food, clothing, music, language. Below: values, assumptions about time, personal space, conflict, authority, gender roles, concepts of fairness
Ethnocentrism assumes your culture is the standard; cultural relativism suspends judgment to understand practices in context. Neither requires approving of all practices.

learning_objectives: Define globalization and trace its historical development
Analyze the effects of globalization on cultural identity
Evaluate the tension between cultural homogenization and cultural preservation
readings: Cultural Anthropology: A Toolkit for a Global Age Ch. 12
Globalization and Culture: Three Scenarios (Nederveen Pieterse, summary)

lecture_notes.introduction: Globalization connects the world through trade, technology, migration, and media. But connection raises a question: does global exchange enrich cultures or erode them?
key_concepts: Globalization: economic, political, cultural, and technological dimensions
Cultural homogenization: the spread of dominant cultures (McDonaldization, Hollywood)
Cultural hybridization: blending of cultures creates new forms (fusion cuisine, K-pop)
Glocalization: global products adapted to local tastes
Indigenous cultural preservation in a globalized world
lecture_notes.demonstration: Mapping cultural exchange: tracing the global origins of items students use daily (clothes, food, technology, music)
setup_requirements: World map poster or digital map tool

review.value: The concern that globalization spreads Western/American culture at the expense of local traditions, languages, and practices. Critics call this cultural imperialism.

Global companies adapt products to local cultures: McDonald's serves McAloo Tikki in India, rice burgers in Japan. Local cultures actively reshape global influences rather than passively receiving them.

learning_objectives: Distinguish between race as a biological concept and as a social construction
Explain how racial and ethnic categories vary across cultures and historical periods
Analyze the real-world consequences of socially constructed racial categories
readings: Cultural Anthropology: A Toolkit for a Global Age Ch. 6
Race: The Power of an Illusion (PBS documentary transcript, selected sections)

lecture_notes.introduction: Race as we commonly understand it does not correspond to meaningful biological divisions in the human species. But as a social construction, it has profoundly shaped history, law, opportunity, and identity across the globe.
key_concepts: Biological consensus: more genetic variation within racial groups than between them
Social construction of race: categories vary by culture and era
Ethnicity vs. race: shared cultural heritage vs. perceived physical characteristics
Historical examples: shifting racial categories in US census history
Real consequences of social categories: disparities in health, wealth, education, justice
lecture_notes.demonstration: Analyzing how the US Census racial categories have changed from 1790 to present and discussing what the changes reveal about social values
setup_requirements: US Census racial category timeline handout (provided)

review.value: A social construction is a concept that exists because humans collectively agree to treat it as real. Race is socially constructed because the boundaries between categories are arbitrary and culturally specific, not biological.
Even though race is socially constructed, racial categories produce real disparities through discrimination, institutional practices, and accumulated historical disadvantage.

learning_objectives: Distinguish between sex, gender, and gender expression
Compare how different cultures construct gender roles and sexuality
Analyze how globalization affects gender norms worldwide
readings: Cultural Anthropology: A Toolkit for a Global Age Ch. 7
Two-Spirit and LGBTQ+ Identities in Indigenous Cultures (supplementary reading)

lecture_notes.introduction: Gender is one of the most fundamental organizing principles of human societies, yet how it is defined, expressed, and enforced varies dramatically across cultures. Studying these variations reveals that much of what we assume is natural is actually cultural.
key_concepts: Sex (biological), gender (social/cultural), gender expression, and sexual orientation as distinct concepts
Gender as performance: Judith Butler's theory of gender performativity
Cross-cultural gender systems: hijra in South Asia, fa'afafine in Samoa, two-spirit in Indigenous North America
Gender inequality: global patterns and cultural variations
Intersectionality: how gender interacts with race, class, and other identities
lecture_notes.demonstration: Comparative analysis of gender role expectations in three different cultural contexts using ethnographic case studies

review.value: Butler argues that gender is not something you are but something you do. It is performed through repeated acts, speech, and behaviors that create the appearance of a stable gender identity.

Coined by Kimberle Crenshaw. A Black woman's experience is not simply 'woman + Black' but a unique position created by the intersection of both identities, producing experiences that neither category alone captures.

class_schedule.topic: Religion, Belief Systems, and Cultural Conflict
learning_objectives: Describe the major world religions and their geographic distribution
Analyze the role of religion in shaping cultural values and social institutions
Evaluate the sources and dynamics of religious and cultural conflict
readings: Cultural Anthropology: A Toolkit for a Global Age Ch. 13
The Clash of Civilizations? (Huntington, summary and critiques)

lecture_notes.introduction: Religion is one of the most powerful forces in human culture. It provides meaning, community, moral frameworks, and identity. It also, at times, becomes a fault line for conflict when different belief systems collide.
key_concepts: Major world religions: Christianity, Islam, Hinduism, Buddhism, Judaism and their global distribution
Religion as a cultural system: rituals, symbols, sacred narratives, and moral codes
Secularization thesis: is religion declining in the modern world?
Religious conflict: when faith intersects with politics, territory, and identity
Interfaith dialogue and models for coexistence
lecture_notes.demonstration: Small group comparative study of how three different religions approach a common ethical question (just war, charity, or environmental stewardship)

review.value: The prediction that modernization would lead to the decline of religion. Evidence is mixed: religion has declined in Western Europe but remains powerful in the US, Latin America, Africa, and Asia.
Religious conflict is rarely about theology alone. It intersects with ethnic identity, territorial claims, economic inequality, and political power. Understanding the full context is essential.

learning_objectives: Identify common barriers to effective intercultural communication
Apply Hofstede's cultural dimensions to understand cross-cultural differences
Develop strategies for building intercultural competence
readings: Intercultural Communication in Contexts Ch. 1-2 by Martin & Nakayama
Hofstede's Cultural Dimensions Theory (summary with examples)

lecture_notes.introduction: In a globalized world, you will work, study, and live alongside people from different cultural backgrounds. Intercultural competence is the ability to communicate effectively and appropriately across cultural boundaries. It is a skill that can be learned.
key_concepts: Hofstede's dimensions: individualism/collectivism, power distance, uncertainty avoidance, masculinity/femininity, long-term/short-term orientation
High-context vs. low-context communication (Edward Hall)
Common barriers: stereotypes, prejudice, ethnocentrism, language differences
Cultural code-switching: adapting behavior across cultural contexts
The developmental model of intercultural sensitivity (Bennett)
lecture_notes.demonstration: Role-play exercise: students navigate a cross-cultural business meeting scenario where cultural misunderstandings create conflict

review.value: High-context cultures (Japan, China, Arab nations) communicate implicitly through context, relationship, and nonverbal cues. Low-context cultures (US, Germany, Scandinavia) rely on explicit, direct verbal communication.
Not about memorizing cultural facts but developing attitudes (curiosity, openness), knowledge (cultural self-awareness and frameworks), and skills (observation, listening, adaptation).

questions.question: Explain the cultural iceberg model and give two examples of 'below the surface' cultural elements that could cause misunderstanding between people from different cultures.
questions.answer: The cultural iceberg model illustrates that the visible parts of culture (food, clothing, language, music, art) are only the tip. The majority of culture lies below the surface: values, beliefs, assumptions about time, personal space, authority, conflict resolution, and concepts of fairness. Example 1: Attitudes toward time. In monochronic cultures (US, Germany), being 15 minutes late to a meeting is disrespectful. In polychronic cultures (parts of Latin America, Middle East), flexible timing is normal and relationships take priority over schedules. This difference causes frustration in international business. Example 2: Directness in communication. In low-context cultures, saying 'no' directly is honest and efficient. In high-context cultures, a direct 'no' is rude; instead, indirect language like 'that might be difficult' conveys refusal. A low-context communicator may miss the rejection entirely.

rubric.key: Correct description of the iceberg model
First below-surface example with misunderstanding scenario
Second below-surface example with misunderstanding scenario

questions.question: Define ethnocentrism and cultural relativism. Then explain why cultural relativism does not mean that all cultural practices must be accepted as morally equivalent.
questions.answer: Ethnocentrism is judging other cultures by the standards of your own culture, assuming your way is the normal or correct way. Cultural relativism is the practice of understanding a culture's practices and beliefs within its own context rather than judging them by outside standards. However, cultural relativism does not require moral approval of all practices. It is a methodological tool for understanding, not a moral position that all practices are equally valid. One can understand why a practice exists in its cultural context while still evaluating it against universal human rights standards. For example, understanding the cultural context of practices that harm individuals does not obligate us to approve of them. Critical evaluation and understanding are not mutually exclusive.

rubric.key: Explains the limits of relativism with reasoning

questions.question: Explain what it means to say that race is a 'social construction' and provide one historical example that demonstrates how racial categories have changed over time.
questions.answer: Saying race is a social construction means that racial categories are created and maintained by human societies rather than being determined by biology. There is more genetic variation within any racial group than between groups, so the boundaries are culturally defined, not biologically meaningful. Historical example: Irish, Italian, and Jewish immigrants to the United States were not considered 'white' in the 19th and early 20th centuries. They faced discrimination, were depicted as racially inferior in media, and were excluded from white social institutions. Over decades, these groups were gradually incorporated into the 'white' category as social boundaries shifted. This demonstrates that racial categories are not fixed biological facts but social agreements that change with time and context.

rubric.key: Valid historical example with specific details
Connects example to the argument that categories change

questions.question: Compare high-context and low-context communication styles using Hofstede's framework. Give one specific workplace scenario where this difference could create a misunderstanding.
questions.answer: High-context communication (common in Japan, China, Korea, and many Arab cultures) conveys meaning through context, relationship, body language, and what is left unsaid. Messages are implicit and indirect. Low-context communication (common in the US, Germany, Australia, and Scandinavia) relies on explicit verbal statements. Messages are direct and literal. Workplace scenario: A Japanese manager is asked by an American colleague for feedback on a proposal. The Japanese manager says 'This is very interesting and has some unique ideas. Perhaps we could explore some additional perspectives.' In Japanese high-context communication, this is a polite rejection suggesting the proposal needs major revision. The American interprets it as mild praise with a suggestion to add a little more content, and submits the proposal unchanged, causing frustration on both sides.

rubric.key: Correct comparison of high and low context
Specific workplace scenario with cultural misunderstanding
Explains how each party interprets differently

major_assignments.title: Cultural Immersion and Analysis Project
major_assignments.description: Engage with a cultural community significantly different from your own through a combination of research, direct experience, and reflection. Attend a cultural event, visit a cultural institution, or conduct an interview with someone from a different cultural background. Then analyze your experience using course concepts and frameworks.
objectives: Engage directly with a cultural perspective different from your own
Apply course concepts (iceberg model, Hofstede's dimensions, ethnocentrism/relativism) to a real experience
Demonstrate intercultural awareness through honest self-reflection
Communicate cultural insights respectfully and accurately
deliverables: Experience documentation: description of the cultural event, institution, or interview (500 words)
Cultural analysis (1000-1500 words) applying at least three course concepts to the experience
Self-reflection (500 words) examining your own cultural assumptions and how the experience challenged them
Visual component: photos, artifacts, or a short video documenting the experience
Reference list with at least 4 sources providing cultural context

criteria: Detailed, specific description of the cultural engagement
Evidence of genuine, respectful participation
Context provided for the cultural practice or community
Visual documentation supports the narrative
At least three course concepts applied accurately
Analysis demonstrates depth, not surface-level observation
Cultural practices understood in their own context
Connections drawn between specific observations and broader cultural patterns
Honest examination of own cultural assumptions
Specific moments of discomfort or surprise analyzed thoughtfully
Growth in intercultural understanding articulated
Avoids stereotyping and generalizing
At least 4 credible sources providing cultural context
Writing is respectful, accurate, and avoids othering language
Professional formatting and organization
Visual component enhances the presentation
Meaningful cultural engagement that goes beyond tourism
Sophisticated application of course concepts to lived experience
Genuine self-reflection demonstrating growth in intercultural competence
Respectful, nuanced writing that avoids stereotypes and generalizations

participation_and_discussion.description: Active, respectful participation in class discussions and activities

quizzes.description: Assessment of cultural concepts, frameworks, and application

weekly_cultural_logs.description: Weekly observations of cultural dynamics in daily life using course concepts

cultural_immersion_project.description: In-depth cultural engagement, analysis, and reflection project

final_portfolio.description: Collection of course work demonstrating growth in intercultural competence

course_policies.attendance: This course relies on diverse perspectives in discussion. Attendance is essential. More than 3 unexcused absences will lower your grade by one letter.
course_policies.late_submissions: Cultural logs are due weekly and cannot be submitted late. The immersion project has staged deadlines with 10% per day late penalty.
course_policies.collaboration: Discussions are collaborative and require respectful engagement with different viewpoints. Written assignments are individual work.
course_policies.academic_integrity: All writing must be your own. Fabricating cultural experiences or interviews is academic dishonesty and grounds for course failure.
course_policies.technology_requirements: Internet access for research. Camera or smartphone for documenting cultural experiences. No specialized software required.
